title: Ephphetha — Be Opened
subtitle: Christ heals the deaf and mute man at the Sea of Galilee

stillness: Draw apart from the crowd now, as he led the man apart.
Lift the treasure of your heart toward heaven.
Be still, and let him touch what is closed in you.
gospel: And again, going out from the borders of Tyre,
Jesus came through Sidon to the Sea of Galilee,
amid the midst of the borders of Decapolis.
And they bring to him a man deaf and mute,
and they beg him to lay his hand upon him.
And taking him aside from the crowd, apart,
he put his fingers into his ears,
and spitting, he touched his tongue.
And looking up to heaven, he groaned,
and said to him: Ephphetha, which is, Be opened.
And immediately his ears were opened,
and the bond of his tongue was loosed,
behold: Behold: this man was not so from birth, nor from a sickness that came upon him,
but from a demon besieging him, and stopping up his hearing and his speech.
And in him three miracles ran together at once:
his hearing was given back, his speech was given back, and from the demon he was set free.
They beg that he lay upon him the all-powerful hand which had made him,
for the hand of Christ is mighty to save.
Whom Jesus touches is healed; whom the Savior touches is healed,
for he himself is salvation and life.
He seeks no herbs from elsewhere: he himself is both the physician and the medicine.
He touches, and heals; he looks, and cures.
See him take the man aside from the crowd, apart,
to teach us to flee empty glory in our deeds.
He puts his fingers into the man's ears,
for the fingers are the gifts of the Holy Spirit; the finger of God is here.
And spitting, he touches the tongue,
for the saliva that flows from the head into the mouth is the divine Wisdom,
which looses the bonds of the lips.
And looking up to heaven he groaned, out of compassion,
taking our cause upon himself, and having mercy on our human nature,
suffering with the misery of so great infirmities, into which the human race fell through sin.
consider: By this deaf and mute man the whole human race is understood:
which had not heard the warnings of salvation, and had ceased from the praise of God.
In the first parent the human race was struck with every plague, with nearly every disease.
It is made blind, while it looks on what must not be seen.
It is made deaf, while it hears what must not be heard.
It falls silent, while it speaks; it is made maimed,
while it raises its hand and stretches it toward the forbidden fruit.
It is bent low, while pride is raised up.
It is made lame, while it goes forward; it is steeped in leprosy, while it is stripped of virtues.
It is filled with the demon, while it grasps after godhead.
It dies, while it boldly excuses its sin.
Yet every sinner, toward God, is deaf,
who stops his ears that he may not hear God speaking to him —
by preaching, by Scripture, by the inward prompting;
for in these three ways God speaks to us, and deaf are they who do not hear him.
And mute are they who do not answer, by thanksgiving, the God who calls them by so many gifts.
But to him whose ears of the heart are opened to obey,
without doubt the bond of his tongue is also loosed,
that the good he himself has done, he may speak as to be done by others.
And rightly it is added: And he spoke rightly.
prayer: Lord Jesus Christ, who at the last forsake the wicked,
come through the Sidon of preaching, to the sea of contrition,
of the Galilee of confession, of satisfaction, and of passing over,
amid the midst of the borders of the Decapolis of charity, advancing in the Decalogue,
and take us aside, apart from the crowd of manifold temptation.
Put the finger of discernment into the ears of our understanding,
and the taste of wisdom into the mouth of our affection,
that the bonds of our tongue may be loosed for confessing,
and that we may speak rightly, with word and deed not at odds with the will,
and that we may shun the praises of men,
so that the deaf may hear through conversion,
and the mute may speak through confession. Amen.

latinJewel.latin: Sanatur quem tangit Jesus, sanatur quem tangit Salvator, quia ipse est salus et vita; non aliunde quærit herbas, ipse est et medicus, et medicina.
latinJewel.english: Whom Jesus touches is healed, whom the Savior touches is healed, for he himself is salvation and life; he seeks no herbs from elsewhere — he himself is both the physician and the medicine.
